blocks.text: Checks for use inside tests. Each check returns a `result`: `ok ()` when it holds, `err message` when it doesn't. A `std/test` case body returns one of these — `pluma test` passes the case when it's `ok` and reports the message when it's `err`. Combine several checks in one case with `assert.all`.
`ok ()` when the two values are equal, compared deeply by value, else `err` showing both sides. The everyday workhorse.
`ok ()` when the two values are *not* equal.
`ok ()` when `value` is `true`.
`ok ()` when `value` is `false`.
`ok ()` when the option holds a value (`some _`).
`ok ()` when the option is `none`.
`ok ()` when the result is a success (`ok _`).
`ok ()` when the result is a failure (`err _`).

Combine many checks into one. `ok ()` when every check is `ok`, otherwise an `err` listing each failure (one per line). All the checks are evaluated — assertions are pure — so a case reports every failure, not just the first.

Base64 encoding for `bytes`: packing raw bytes into a text string made of letters, digits, and a couple of symbols, so binary data can ride safely through things that only handle text -- email, JSON, URLs.
There are two flavors: - `encode` / `decode` use the standard alphabet (with `+` and `/`)
- `url-encode` / `url-decode` use the URL-safe alphabet (`-` and `_`

spans.text: ) with no padding, so the result

safe to
drop straight into a URL

blocks.text: Both decoders accept input with or without the trailing `=` padding, and reject any character outside the chosen alphabet.

Why a `decode` failed: the input's length can't spell a whole number of bytes, or it holds a character outside the chosen alphabet. A precise enum (rather than a string) so a caller can tell the two apart -- and, via its `describe` instance, erase into `std/error` and read well in a message.
Encodes bytes with the standard alphabet, including `=` padding.
Encodes bytes with the URL-safe alphabet and no padding.
Decodes a standard-alphabet base64 string back into bytes. The trailing padding is optional.
Decodes a URL-safe base64 string back into bytes. Padding is optional.

Bitwise operations on whole numbers. A Pluma `int` is a 64-bit signed integer (two's complement), and these functions treat it as a flat row of 64 bits -- handy for flags, masks, packing several small values into one number, and low-level byte twiddling.

Each function also has operator syntax: `&` (and), `|` (or), `^` (xor), `<<` (shift-left), `>>` (shift-right), `>>>` (shift-right-unsigned), and the prefix `~` (not). So `x & mask | flag` is `bit.bitwise-or (bit.bitwise-and x mask) flag`. The operators bind tighter than comparison (so `x & mask == 0` means `(x & mask) == 0`); the functions stay useful for `|>` chains and passing a bit op as a value.
Bitwise AND: a 1 in the result wherever *both* inputs have a 1. The usual tool for masking -- keeping only the bits a mask selects.
Bitwise OR: a 1 in the result wherever *either* input has a 1. The usual tool for setting flags.
Bitwise XOR ("exclusive or"): a 1 wherever the inputs *differ*. Toggles the bits that the second operand has set.
Bitwise NOT: flips every bit. Because ints are two's complement, this is the same as `0 - n - 1`, so `not 0` is -1 and `not 5` is -6.
Shift the bits left by `n` places, filling the low end with zeros. Each step doubles the value, so a left shift by `n` multiplies by 2^n.

Shift the bits right by `n` places, *preserving the sign* (the high bit is copied in). This is an arithmetic shift: for non-negative numbers it divides by 2^n rounding toward negative infinity, and negatives stay negative.
Shift the bits right by `n` places, filling the high end with zeros (a logical shift). Treats the number as an unsigned 64-bit pattern, so a negative input becomes a large positive result.

Working with raw bytes. A `bytes` is a sequence of 8-bit values (0-255 each) -- the form data takes on disk and over the network. It's the right tool for binary content (an image, a compressed file, a network frame) or for text whose encoding you don't control.

Write a bytes literal with single quotes, using `\xNN` to give a byte by its two-digit hex value: `'hello'`, `'\x00\xff'`. (Double quotes make a `string` instead.) When the bytes happen to be UTF-8 text, `to-string` decodes them; `string.to-bytes` goes the other way.
Like strings and lists, bytes never change in place.
How many bytes are in the sequence.
True when there are no bytes at all.
The byte at position `i` (counting from 0), as an int in 0-255 wrapped in `some`. `none` if `i` is out of range.

The byte at position `i`, as an int in 0-255. The unchecked primitive the byte operations below build on: partial, so a position outside `0 <= i < length` stops the program. Use `at` when `i` might be out of range -- it returns an `option` instead.

Builds an `n`-byte sequence by calling `f` with each index `0..n-1`; `f`'s int result is taken mod 256. One O(n) pass -- the byte-buffer counterpart to `list.build`, and what the builders below ride on.
Joins two byte sequences end to end into a new one.
The slice from position `start` (counted in) up to `end` (counted out). Positions past either end are pulled back to that end rather than erroring.
True when `needle` appears somewhere inside `haystack`. Scans for the first match the way `split` does (`matches-at`, below), stopping early.
True when the bytes begin with `prefix`. Handy for spotting a file by its magic number -- a PNG starts with byte 0x89 then "PNG".
True when the bytes end with `suffix`.
Repeats the bytes `n` times, joined into one sequence.
Returns the bytes in reverse order.
Spreads the bytes out into a list of ints, one per byte (each 0-255).
Packs a list of ints back into bytes. Each int has to fit in a byte (0-255); `err` if one doesn't.
Glues a list of byte sequences together, dropping `separator` between each pair.

Glues every part into one byte array in a single pass: sums the lengths, allocates the result once, and blits each part with `sep` between -- O(total) copying and one allocation (versus the O(k) intermediate arrays a `concat` fold or tree would build).
Breaks the bytes into pieces at every occurrence of `separator`.
Decodes the bytes as UTF-8 text. `ok s` on success, `err` if the bytes aren't valid UTF-8.
std/css -- typed CSS values and declarations.

The pure, tier-neutral value layer of the styling story. You build typed colors and lengths (`css.hex`, `css.rem`, `css.px`) and combine them into declarations (`css.color`, `css.padding`), then serialize a list of declarations to an inline `style` string with `css.inline`. The win over a hand-written `view.style "color: red; padding: 1rem"` is type-safety and units you can't typo.

This module is `std/*` (tier-neutral, no host capabilities), so it produces the same strings on the server (SSR, via `view.to-string`) and in the browser. The higher tiers -- extracted `css.rule` stylesheets and reactive custom properties -- build on these same value types later; nothing here touches the DOM.
Pair it with `std/view`'s `style-of` (a declaration list as a `style` attribute) and `classes` (a conditional class list); both lower to plain string attributes.

A CSS color value. Build with `hex`/`rgb`/`rgba`/`named` rather than naming the constructors; public (not opaque) only so it can appear in signatures. (Named `color-value`, not `color`, so it doesn't clash with the `css.color` property builder -- types and defs share one namespace.)
A CSS length/size value: a number paired with its unit (`16px`, `1.5rem`, `50%`), or the `auto` keyword. Build with `px`/`rem`/`em`/`pct`/`vh`/`vw`, or `auto`.

An entry in a style block. Usually a single declaration -- a property name and its already-serialized value (`color: #fff`), built with the property helpers (`css.color`, `css.padding`, ...) or the generic `css.property`. Inside a `css.rule` it may also be a nested pseudo-class (`css.on "hover" [...]`) or media (`css.media "(...)" [...]`) block, which sit as siblings of the plain declarations -- there is no separate base wrapper. `css.inline`/`view.style-of` serialize the plain declarations and ignore any nested blocks (an inline style can't carry them).
A hex color. The leading `#` is optional -- `hex "#e11"` and `hex "e11"` both render as `#e11`.
An opaque RGB color from three 0-255 channels: `rgb 37 99 235` => `rgb(37, 99, 235)`.
An RGB color with an alpha (0.0-1.0): `rgba 0 0 0 0.5` => `rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.5)`.
A named CSS color, passed through verbatim: `named "rebeccapurple"`.

A reference to a CSS custom property (`var(--accent)`) -- the bridge for model-driven values into an otherwise-static extracted rule. The live value is supplied separately (by `view.vars`); here a rule just names it: `css.background (css.var "accent")` => `background: var(--accent)`.
Pixels: `px 16.0` => `16px`.
Root-em (relative to the root font size): `rem 1.5` => `1.5rem`.
Em (relative to the element's font size): `em 1.25` => `1.25em`.
Percent: `pct 50.0` => `50%`.
Viewport height units: `vh 100.0` => `100vh`.
Viewport width units: `vw 50.0` => `50vw`.
The `auto` keyword as a length, for browser-chosen sizing (`width auto`) and the centering idiom (`margin-inline auto`): renders `auto`.
Text color: `css.color (css.hex "#e11")` => `color: #e11`.
Background: `css.background (css.hex "#fff")` => `background: #fff`.
Border color: `css.border-color (css.hex "#ccc")` => `border-color: #ccc`.
Padding on all sides: `css.padding (css.rem 1.0)` => `padding: 1rem`.
Padding above the content: `css.padding-top (css.rem 1.0)` => `padding-top: 1rem`.
Padding to the right of the content: `css.padding-right (css.rem 1.0)`.
Padding below the content: `css.padding-bottom (css.rem 1.0)`.
Padding to the left of the content: `css.padding-left (css.rem 1.0)`.

Padding on the block axis -- top and bottom together (in the default writing mode): `css.padding-block (css.rem 1.0)` => `padding-block: 1rem`. For an asymmetric top/bottom pair, use `padding-top`/`padding-bottom`.

Padding on the inline axis -- left and right together (in the default writing mode): `css.padding-inline (css.rem 1.5)` => `padding-inline: 1.5rem`. For an asymmetric left/right pair, use `padding-left`/`padding-right`.
Margin on all sides: `css.margin (css.rem 1.0)` => `margin: 1rem`.
Margin above the element: `css.margin-top (css.rem 1.0)` => `margin-top: 1rem`.
Margin to the right of the element: `css.margin-right (css.rem 1.0)`.
Margin below the element: `css.margin-bottom (css.rem 1.0)`.
Margin to the left of the element: `css.margin-left (css.rem 1.0)`.

Margin on the block axis -- top and bottom together (in the default writing mode): `css.margin-block (css.rem 1.0)` => `margin-block: 1rem`. For an asymmetric top/bottom pair, use `margin-top`/`margin-bottom`.

Margin on the inline axis -- left and right together (in the default writing mode): `css.margin-inline css.auto` centers a fixed-width block (`margin-inline: auto`). For an asymmetric left/right pair, use `css.margin-left`/`css.margin-right`.
Width: `css.width (css.px 240.0)` => `width: 240px`.
Height: `css.height (css.px 240.0)` => `height: 240px`.
Minimum height: `css.min-height (css.rem 22.0)` => `min-height: 22rem`.
Maximum height: `css.max-height (css.vh 100.0)` => `max-height: 100vh`.
Minimum width: `css.min-width (css.rem 12.0)` => `min-width: 12rem`.
Maximum width: `css.max-width (css.rem 40.0)` => `max-width: 40rem`.
Gap between flex/grid items: `css.gap (css.rem 0.5)` => `gap: 0.5rem`.
Font size: `css.font-size (css.rem 1.25)` => `font-size: 1.25rem`.
Spacing between letters, often a small `em` (and frequently negative to tighten headings): `css.letter-spacing (css.em -0.02)` => `letter-spacing: -0.02em`.
Corner rounding: `css.border-radius (css.px 6.0)` => `border-radius: 6px`.
Offset from the top edge when positioned: `css.top (css.px 0.0)`.
Offset from the right edge when positioned: `css.right (css.px 0.0)`.
Offset from the bottom edge when positioned: `css.bottom (css.px 0.0)`.
Offset from the left edge when positioned: `css.left (css.px 0.0)`.
Extra space kept above an element when it is scrolled into view by an anchor jump (so a sticky header doesn't cover it): `css.scroll-margin-top (css.rem 1.5)`.
Opacity (0.0-1.0): `opacity 0.5` => `opacity: 0.5`.
Stacking order: `z-index 10` => `z-index: 10`.
Line height as a unitless multiple: `line-height 1.5` => `line-height: 1.5`.
Width a tab character renders as, in spaces: `tab-size 2` => `tab-size: 2`.
Layout mode: `css.display "flex"` => `display: flex`.
Positioning scheme: `css.position "relative"` => `position: relative`.
Horizontal text alignment: `css.text-align "center"`.
Main-axis direction of a flex container: `css.flex-direction "column"`.
Main-axis alignment in a flex container: `css.justify-content "center"`.
Cross-axis alignment in a flex container: `css.align-items "center"`.
Font weight: `css.font-weight "bold"` => `font-weight: bold`.
Font family: `css.font-family "serif"` => `font-family: serif`.
Text decoration: `css.text-decoration "underline"`.
Mouse cursor over the element: `css.cursor "pointer"`.
How overflowing content is handled: `css.overflow "hidden"`.

A raw property/value pair -- the escape hatch for any property without its own builder: `css.property "grid-template-columns" "1fr 1fr"`. Still us producing the bytes (one declaration), not the untrusted-CSS seam (`css.raw`, deferred).

Serialize a declaration list to an inline `style` string: `inline [color (hex "#e11"), padding (rem 1.0)]` => `"color: #e11; padding: 1rem"`. This is what `std/view`'s `style-of` wraps into a `style` attribute; you can also call it directly inside `view.bind-attr "style" (fun { ... })` for a reactive style. Only plain declarations are serialized -- any nested `on`/`media` block (which can appear in a `css.rule` list but not in an inline style) is skipped.

A complete style rule -- a flat list of declarations and nested blocks scoped to one hashed class. The leading string is an optional *debug name*: empty in a normal build, set to the source `def` name under `pluma dev` (see `label`) so the generated class reads `card-<hash>` in devtools instead of a bare `c<hash>`. It never enters the content hash, so dedup and the rendered CSS are unaffected.
Assemble a ruleset from its entries -- plain declarations and any `on`/`media` blocks, mixed freely in one list.

Tag a ruleset with a *debug name* so its generated class reads `name-<hash>` (e.g. `card-1a2b3c4`) rather than a bare `c<hash>` -- friendlier to spot in browser devtools. The name only prefixes the class; it never enters the content hash, so the rendered CSS and dedup are unchanged. `pluma dev` injects this automatically around a top-level `css.ruleset` def, passing the def's own name; a normal build carries no name and the class stays `c<hash>`. You rarely call it by hand.

A pseudo-class (or pseudo-element) variant: `on "hover" [...]` => `.<class>:hover { ... }`. Pass the suffix after the colon (`"hover"`, `"focus"`, `"disabled"`); a pseudo-element needs the leading colon yourself (`":before"`).
A media-query variant: `media "(max-width: 600px)" [...]` => `@media (max-width: 600px) { .<class> { ... } }`.

Merge several rulesets into one: all their blocks are concatenated, so a later rule's declarations win over an earlier one's (single source order, one hash, one class). The order-explicit way to combine styles -- preferred over stacking two `view.styled` attributes on a node, whose conflicts resolve by stylesheet order.

The class name for a ruleset, AND register its rendered CSS in the collector so a later `collect`/`style-tag` includes it (idempotent -- two identical rules share a class and one entry). This is what `view.styled` calls; call it directly only if you are assembling a `class` string by hand.

All collected CSS as one stylesheet string -- every rule registered (via `class-of`/`view.styled`) since the last `reset`. Server-side rendering inlines this into a `<style>` tag (see `style-tag`); a build step can instead write it to an `app.css` file. Order is collector-determined and stable for a given rule set.
`collect` wrapped in a `<style>` element, for inlining into a server-rendered document `<head>`. The CSS is ours (a trusted producer), so it is not escaped.
Clear the collector -- for tests and for resetting between independent renders.
One stop in an animation: an offset (`"from"`/`"to"`/`"50%"`) and its declarations.
An animation's stops, a pure value. (Type `keyframe-set`, not `keyframes`, since the `css.keyframes` builder takes that name.)
One animation stop: `css.frame "from" [...]`, `css.frame "50%" [...]`.
Bundle stops into an animation value.

An `animation` declaration that plays `kf` with the given timing (`"1s linear infinite"`), AND registers `kf` as an `@keyframes` block in the collector. `css.animation spin "1s linear infinite"` => `animation: <name> 1s linear infinite`.
One global rule: a raw selector (`":root"`, `"*"`, `"@font-face"`) and its declarations. The selector is emitted verbatim, so any at-rule with a declaration body works.
A global rule: `css.at ":root" [...]`, `css.at "@font-face" [...]`.
Register global rules into the collector (`selector { decls }`, unscoped). Returns nothing -- it's a setup call; run it before `collect`/`style-tag`.

The collector's revision -- it increments each time a new rule is registered or the collector is `reset`, and is otherwise stable. Re-registering an already-collected rule does not change it. Built for a `<style>`-syncing consumer to skip re-rendering the stylesheet on the common flush that added no new rule.

Dictionaries: a collection of key/value pairs you can look things up in by key. `dict k v` is read "a dict from `k` to `v`" -- `dict string int` maps strings to whole numbers, `dict int bool` maps numbers to true/false, and so on.
Unlike a list, a dict finds a value by its key rather than its position, and each key appears at most once. Inserting a key that's already there replaces the old value.

Keys can be any type that knows how to `hash` itself -- the built-in types (`int`, `float`, `string`, `bytes`, `bool`) all do, and your own enums can by `implement`ing the `hash` trait. The functions that look a key up (`insert`, `lookup`, `remove`, `update`, `contains-key`, `from-entries`, `merge`) carry a `where (hash k)` constraint that says so.

A dict is an immutable value: `insert`, `remove`, and `update` return a *new* dict and leave the original untouched (the way a record `{ ...r, f: v }` does), so two names for the same dict never interfere. `map`, `filter`, and `merge` likewise build a new dict.
Iteration order is unspecified: `keys`, `values`, `entries`, `map`, `filter`, and `fold` walk the pairs in an arbitrary (hash-determined) order.
An empty dict with no entries. The starting point you build up from with `insert`.

items.name: insert
items.signature: (dict a b) a b -> dict a b

blocks.text: Returns a new dict with `key` set to `value`; the original is left unchanged. If the key was already present its value is replaced.
Looks up `key`, returning `some value` if it's present and `none` if it isn't. Pair it with `??` to supply a fallback: `(dict.lookup d k) ?? 0`.

items.name: remove
items.signature: (dict a b) a -> dict a b

blocks.text: Returns a new dict without `key`; the original is left unchanged. Removing a key that isn't there returns an equivalent dict.

items.name: update
items.signature: (dict a b) a ((option b) -> b) -> dict a b

blocks.text: Returns a new dict with `key` updated by running `f` on what's there now -- `some value` if the key is present, `none` if it isn't -- and storing whatever `f` returns. A fused read-modify-write: it finds the key just once, where a separate `lookup` then `insert` would find it twice.

spans.text: # count occurrences: bump the running tally, starting from 0

blocks.text: Returns a fresh empty dict (equivalent to `dict.empty ()`; provided for symmetry with the other operations).
True when `key` has a value in the dict, false otherwise. Same answer as checking whether `lookup` returned `some`, when you don't need the value itself.
Returns how many key/value pairs are in the dict.
Returns all the keys as a list, in an unspecified order.
Returns all the values as a list, in the same (unspecified) order as `keys`.
Returns every pair as a list of `(key, value)` tuples, in an unspecified order. The inverse of `from-entries`.
Builds a dict from a list of `(key, value)` tuples. Later pairs win when a key appears more than once. The inverse of `entries`.

items.name: merge
items.signature: (dict a b) (dict a b) -> dict a b

blocks.text: Combines two dicts into a brand-new dict, leaving both inputs alone. When the same key is in both, the value from the second dict (`right`) wins.

items.name: map
items.signature: (dict a b) (b -> c) -> dict a c

blocks.text: Runs `f` on every value and collects the results into a new dict, keeping the same keys. The result values can be a different type than you started with. The original dict is left alone.

items.name: filter
items.signature: (dict a b) (a b -> bool) -> dict a b

blocks.text: Keeps only the pairs that `pred` returns true for, dropping the rest, in a new dict. The predicate receives both the key and the value; the original dict is left alone.

items.name: fold
items.signature: (dict a b) c (c a b -> c) -> c

blocks.text: Boils the whole dict down to a single value by combining the pairs one at a time, in an unspecified order.

You hand it three things: the dict, a starting value, and a function `f acc key value`. Pluma walks the pairs and at each one calls `f` with the running total so far, the key, and the value; whatever `f` returns becomes the new running total.
Summing all the values starts the total at 0:
Errors that erase late and carry their own trace.

Pluma gives you two ways to model failure, and this module is the bridge between them. At the leaf, keep a precise, closed enum -- the kind `when` can match exhaustively, so callers can react to a specific failure. At a boundary, where you only want to propagate or report, erase that precise type into the single `error` carrier here. You "erase late": your signature stays `result a db-error` until the point you stop caring which failure it was, and `try` does the one-way compression at that seam.

An `error` is a chain of frames -- a message plus an optional cause beneath it. Each `try` that propagates into an `error` context pushes a frame recording where it went, so the chain reads like a stack trace that (unlike a native one) survives the async boundaries Pluma's scheduler crosses, because it is plain data. Build depth by hand with `context`:

Erasing works for `result` and `task` alike, so the same `try` reads the same whether a step is sync or async. A type erases into `error` exactly when it has a `describe` instance -- and `string` has one, so every stdlib API that fails with a `string` (`fs`, `db`, `http`, `sql`, ...) erases for free; a `remote` call's `rpc-error` does too. Convention: a *library* exposes a precise enum (or a string) so its own callers can react; an *app* declares `result _ error` / `task _ error` at the layer it stops caring which failure it was, and lets `try` compress. `error` is the app-layer carrier, not a return type to reach for everywhere -- keep a signature precise as long as someone matches it.

A value that can describe itself for a human. It is consumed only at the point a precise error is erased into `error` (ordinary static dispatch, like `to-string` or `compare`) -- never stored or recovered -- so erasing a type into `error` is a deliberate opt-in: give it a `describe` instance.

One hop in a failure's history: a message for this level, the cause it wrapped (if any), and the global constructor id of the precise value it was erased from (or -1 when there was none -- a bare `new`, or a `context` wrapper). The id rides only the leaf frame; `is-a` reads it back to classify the failure. Opaque -- build one with `new`/`from`, add levels with `context`, and read it back with `message`/`trace`/`is-a`.
A fresh error from a plain message, with no cause beneath it.

Erases a precise error value into the carrier, rendering it through its `describe` instance and remembering its constructor id so `is` can classify it later. This is what `try` calls for you when it propagates a precise failure into an `error`-typed function; reach for it directly only when erasing by hand.

Wraps an error in another layer of context, putting `m` on top and the original underneath. This is how a chain grows: the outermost frame is the most recent, the leaf the root cause. The wrapper carries no constructor id of its own -- `is` still sees the leaf's.
Renders the whole chain as one line, outermost frame first and joined with `": "` -- the readable one-liner for a log or a message to the user.
The frames as a list, outermost first -- the structured form of `message`, for logging each level on its own or inspecting the chain.

True when the failure ultimately came from `variant` -- the specific leaf constructor it was erased from -- no matter how much `context` was layered on top. Pass the constructor you're testing against:

It compares constructor identity only, never the payload, so a variant carrying data still matches any value built from the same constructor. A non-variant `variant` (or an error that never carried one) matches nothing. (Named `is-a`, not `is`, because `is` is a reserved keyword.)
std/event -- a DOM event delivered to a view handler.

This is a *shared* (`std/*`) module, not `std/web/*`, on purpose: `std/view` names the `event` type in every handler's signature, and `std/view` must build on the server profile too (server-side rendering calls `view.to-string`). So the event *type* and its accessors live here, tier-neutral, while the underlying host builtins are browser-only -- they are only ever *called* inside a handler, which the server's `to-string` drops, so a server build never reaches them.

An `event` is opaque, like a `dom.node`: the browser owns it, you hold the handle and read details with the accessors below. `std/web/dom` re-exports this same type for `add-listener`, so a handler built by `std/view` and a listener wired by `std/web/render` agree on one event type.

An event delivered to a handler -- a click, an input change, a form submit. Opaque; read details with the accessors below. (The runtime carrier is an engine `externref`, not the `int` -- the payload is a formality so the type is a real opaque enum.)
The `.value` of the element the event fired on (e.g. the text an `input` now holds). Empty string when there's none.
Whether the element the event fired on is `checked` -- for a checkbox/radio `change` handler. The two-way `view.checked` binding reads it to write its signal.
Suppress the browser's default action for this event -- e.g. stop a form `submit` from reloading the page.

Hex (base-16) encoding for `bytes`: turning raw bytes into a text string of hex digits, and back again. Each byte becomes exactly two digits, so the text is always twice as long as the bytes it came from. Output is lowercase; the decoder accepts either case.

Why a `decode` failed: an odd number of digits (each byte needs two), or a character that isn't a hex digit. A precise enum (rather than a string) lets a caller tell the two apart, and its `describe` instance erases it into `std/error` and renders it for a message.
Encodes bytes as a lowercase hex string -- two digits per byte.

Decodes a hex string back into bytes. The input must have an even number of digits and contain only 0-9 and a-f (either case); anything else -- a stray space, a "0x" prefix, an odd length -- gives `err`.

Reading and writing JSON. `parse` turns JSON text into a `value` you can pick apart; `stringify` turns a `value` back into text. Parsing never crashes -- it hands back a `result`, so malformed input is just an `err` you handle like any other.

A JSON number can be a whole number or a decimal, and Pluma keeps the two apart (`value.int` vs `value.float`) instead of forcing everything to a float, which would lose precision on large whole numbers. The `get-number` helper smooths over the distinction when you don't care.

A parsed JSON value. JSON has a handful of shapes -- null, a boolean, a number, a string, an array, or an object -- and a `value` is exactly one of them. `parse` produces these, and the `get-*` helpers below pull a plain Pluma value back out. You can also build one directly: `json.value.string "hi"`, `json.value.array [...]`, and so on.
What `parse` returns when the text isn't valid JSON. `line` and `col` point at the spot where parsing gave up (both counting from 1), and `message` says what went wrong.

items.name: parse
items.signature: string -> result value ({col: int, line: int, message: string})

blocks.text: Parses JSON text into a `value`. Returns `ok v` on success, or, on malformed input, `err {line, col, message}` pointing at the problem. Never crashes.

spans.text: # => err {line: 1, col: 3, message: "key must be a string at line 1 column 3"}

blocks.text: Renders a `value` back to compact JSON text -- no extra spaces, everything on one line. Object keys come out in the order they went in.
Like `stringify`, but laid out for people to read: a 2-space indent and one item per line. Good for config files or debug output.
produces the string:
True when the value is JSON `null`.
Looks up a key on a JSON object, handing back its value in `some`. `none` if the value isn't an object, or the key isn't present.
Pulls a whole number out of a value. `some n` if the value is a JSON integer, `none` for anything else (a float included).
Pulls a decimal number out. Strict: a JSON integer gives `none` here -- use `get-number` if you'll take either kind of number.
Pulls any number out, turning a JSON integer into a float so you don't have to check which kind it was. Slightly lossy for whole numbers bigger than about 9 quadrillion (2^53).
Pulls a boolean out of a value.
Pulls a string out of a value.
Pulls the contents of a JSON array out as a `list value`. `none` if the value isn't an array.
Pulls the contents of a JSON object out as a `dict string value`, which you can walk with `std/dict`. `none` if the value isn't an object.
std/keyed -- ready-made key functions for `view.each` (and any keyed API).
`view.each` reconciles a list by a stable string key per item -- a `fun a -> string`. When the key is just a field, these spare you the lambda:

Each reads one field and renders it with `to-string`, so it works for any field type (an int `id`, a string `name`, ...). They aren't tied to one record type: `by-id` applies to *any* record carrying an `id`, whatever its other fields, so one helper serves every keyed list in your app.
Key by the item's `id` field -- the usual choice for records with a unique id.
Key by the item's `name` field.
Key by the item's `key` field.
Key by the whole item -- for a list whose elements are themselves the keys (a `list string`, a `list int`): `view.each tags keyed.by-value chip`.

Lists: ordered collections where every element has the same type. `list a` is read "a list of `a`s" -- `list int` is a list of whole numbers, `list string` a list of strings, and so on. You write one with square brackets: `[1, 2, 3]`.
Lists never change in place. Everything here that "adds", "removes", or "sorts" actually hands you back a brand-new list and leaves the original alone.

Some of these functions take a *predicate*: a small function you pass in that looks at one element and answers true or false (`fun n { n > 0 }`). Others take a transform (`fun n { n * 2 }`). Writing a function inline like that is the normal way to tell a list operation what to do.

When a lookup might come up empty -- asking for the first element of a list that could be empty, say -- you get back an `option` (see `std/option`) rather than a crash: `some value` if it found something, `none` if it didn't.
Returns how many elements are in the list.
Returns the element at position `i`, counting from 0. This is the primitive O(1) access the iterators below are built on.

It is *partial*: a position outside `0 <= i < length` stops the program. When `i` might be out of range, reach for a total operation instead -- `head` for the first element, `find` to search by value.

Overwrites the element at position `i` in place, returning `nothing`. This is the one mutation Pluma allows on a list -- normally lists are immutable and every operation hands back a fresh list. An in-place set is the deliberate escape hatch for when you're filling or rearranging a buffer slot by slot (it's what `sort` is built on). Partial like `get`: a position outside `0 <= i < length` stops the program.

Because a list's storage is shared, a `set` is visible through *every* binding pointing at the same list -- so use it only on a list you just built and still solely own. When in doubt, build a fresh list instead (`build`/`map`/spread); that's the immutable norm.

Appends `x` to the end of `xs` in place, in amortized O(1) time -- the efficient way to grow a list whose final size you don't know up front (accumulating in a loop, collecting matches, parsing). Without it the only option is `[...acc, x]` / `list.concat acc [x]`, which copy the whole list each step and so cost O(n^2) overall.

Like `set`, this is the deliberate mutable escape hatch: the same aliasing caveat applies -- the append is visible through *every* binding pointing at the same list, so use it only on a list you just built and still solely own (the fresh-accumulator pattern below). When in doubt, build a fresh list.

Removes the last element of `xs` in place and returns it as `some last`, or `none` if the list is empty. The O(1) counterpart to `push`: together they make a list a fast last-in-first-out stack (`push` to add, `pop` to take the most recent back). Use `last` to look at the end without removing it.
Like `push`, this is a mutable escape hatch -- the removal is visible through *every* binding pointing at the same list -- so use it on a list you solely own.

Builds `[f 0, f 1, ..., f (n-1)]` in a single O(n) pass: `f` is called with each index in turn and its results collected in order. The tabulating counterpart to `fold`, and the primitive the list builders below (`map`, `take`, ...) are written on top of.
True when the list has no elements at all. Just a shorthand for checking whether `length` is zero.
Returns a new list with the elements in the opposite order.
Joins two lists end to end into a new one: every element of the first, then every element of the second.
When you're building a list inline, the `...` spread does the same thing and reads more cleanly: `[...xs, ...ys]`.
True when `target` shows up somewhere in the list. Elements are compared by value, so `[1, 2, 3]` contains `2`, and a list of strings contains a string when one of them spells the same.

Runs `f` on every element and collects the results into a new list, keeping the order. The results can be a different type than you started with -- run a list of numbers through a function that returns strings and you get a list of strings.
Keeps only the elements that `pred` returns true for, dropping the rest. Order is preserved.
Boils the whole list down to a single value by combining the elements one at a time, left to right.

You hand it three things: the list, a starting value, and a function `f acc element`. Pluma walks the list front to back, and at each element calls `f` with the running total so far (the "accumulator") and that element; whatever `f` returns becomes the new running total. When the elements run out, the final total is the answer.
Summing a list starts the total at 0 and adds each element:
The accumulator can be any type, not just a number -- start with "" and you can glue the elements together into a string.
Runs `f` on every element just for the side effects -- printing each one, say -- and returns `nothing`. If you actually want the results `f` produces, reach for `map` instead.

spans.text: # prints a, then b on separate lines

blocks.text: Returns the first element, wrapped in `some`. An empty list has no first element, so you get `none`.
Returns everything except the first element, wrapped in `some`; `none` for an empty list. Note that the tail of a one-element list is `some []` -- an empty list, not `none`.
Returns a new list with just the first `n` elements. If the list is shorter than `n`, you get the whole thing back.
Returns a new list with the first `n` elements removed. If `n` is at least as long as the list, you get an empty list.
Returns the first element that `pred` returns true for, wrapped in `some`. If nothing matches, `none`.
True when at least one element passes `pred`. Stops as soon as it finds one.
True when every element passes `pred`. An empty list counts as true -- there's nothing in it to fail the test.
Returns the last element, wrapped in `some`; `none` for an empty list. The mirror of `head`.
Builds the ascending list of integers from `start` up to but not including `end`. When `end` isn't past `start` the range is empty.
Pairs up two lists element by element. The result is as long as the shorter input -- extra elements of the longer one are dropped.
The inverse of `zip`: splits a list of pairs into a pair of lists, the firsts and the seconds, each in order.

Runs `f` on every element -- where `f` returns a *list* -- and concatenates all those lists into one, in order. Use it when each element expands into zero or more results (the "map, then flatten" pattern).

items.name: try-map
items.signature: (list a) (a -> result b c) -> result (list b) c

blocks.text: Like `map`, but the mapping function can fail. Applies `f` to each element, collecting the `ok` values into a list; the first `err` short-circuits and is returned as-is (no further elements are tried). The fallible cousin of `map`, handy for decoding a whole list where any element might be malformed.
Concatenates a list of lists into a single flat list, preserving order. One level deep only -- `[[1, 2], [3], [4, 5]]` becomes `[1, 2, 3, 4, 5]`.
Returns the index of the first element for which `pred` is true, wrapped in `some`; `none` if nothing matches. The position-returning companion to `find` (which returns the element itself).
Returns the index of the first element equal to `target` (by structural `==`), wrapped in `some`; `none` if it isn't present. The position-returning companion to `contains`.
Counts how many elements pass `pred`. Like `filter` followed by `length`, but without building the intermediate list.

Returns the longest run of elements from the front that all pass `pred`, stopping at the first that doesn't. Unlike `filter`, it stops at the first failure rather than scanning the whole list, so everything after that boundary is dropped even if it would pass.
The other half of `take-while`: drops the leading run that passes `pred` and returns everything from the first failure onward (that failing element included).

Splits a list in two by a predicate, in one pass over the input: the first list holds the elements that pass `pred`, the second the ones that don't, each keeping its original order. Returns the pair `(passing, failing)`.

Drops later duplicates, keeping the first occurrence of each element (so order is preserved). Elements are compared with structural `==`, the same equality `contains` uses, so this works on any type without extra setup.
It compares each element against the ones already kept, so the cost grows with the square of the length -- fine for modest lists; for large ones, sort first or build a `dict`.
Pairs each element with its index, counting from 0. Useful when a transform or filter needs to know *where* it is in the list.

Breaks a list into consecutive sublists of length `size`. The last chunk is shorter if the length isn't an exact multiple. A `size` of zero or less yields an empty list (there's no sensible chunking).
Inserts `sep` between every pair of adjacent elements (not before the first or after the last). A list of fewer than two elements comes back unchanged.
Returns a new list with the elements in sorted order. You choose the order with a compare function `cmp a b` that reports whether `a` belongs before `b`, after it, or in the same place.
Usually you don't have to write one. `ord.compare` already knows how to order the built-in types -- numbers smallest-first, strings alphabetically -- so just hand it in:
To sort the other way, swap the two arguments in your own compare function:
The sort is *stable*: elements your compare function calls `eq` keep their original order relative to each other.

Sorts by a *key* drawn from each element rather than by comparing the elements directly: `key` maps an element to something orderable, and the list is sorted by those keys (ascending). This is the common case -- sorting records by one field -- without writing a compare function.
The key is recomputed on each comparison, so keep it cheap. To sort descending, sort ascending and `reverse`, or drop down to `sort` with your own compare function.
Returns the element whose key is smallest, or `none` if the list is empty. `key` maps each element to something orderable, the same way `sort-by` does. On ties the earliest such element wins.
Returns the element whose key is largest, or `none` if the list is empty -- the mirror of `min-by`. On ties the earliest such element wins.

Returns the smallest element, or `none` for an empty list (which has no minimum). Works on anything orderable -- numbers, strings, and the rest. When you want the smallest *by a key* rather than by the whole element, reach for `min-by`.
Returns the largest element, or `none` for an empty list -- the mirror of `min`. For the largest *by a key*, see `max-by`.
Adds up all the elements, or `none` for an empty list. Works on both `int` and `float` lists (anything `numeric`).
It's `none` on empty rather than zero because there's no single zero that fits every numeric type -- pair it with `??` to pick one:

spans.text: # total, or 0.0 if there were none

blocks.text: Multiplies all the elements together, or `none` for an empty list -- the multiplicative companion to `sum`. As with `sum`, supply the identity (here `1`) with `??`:
A task-local cell. You never see its contents directly -- make one with `local.new`, read it with `local.get`, bind it with `local.with`.
Makes a new cell whose default is `initial` -- the value `local.get` returns whenever it's read outside any enclosing `local.with`. Declare one once at the top level, like a `ref`:
Reads the value bound for the current task: the nearest enclosing `local.with` on this branch of the task tree, or the cell's default if there is none.

items.name: with
items.signature: (local a) a (nothing -> task b c) -> task b c

blocks.text: Runs `body` with the cell bound to `val`, for the body and every task it spawns; restores the previous binding on the way out (by any path -- success, failure, or cancellation). The binding is dynamically scoped: a nested `with` on the same cell shadows this one within its own subtree, and concurrent siblings each keep the value bound on their own branch.

blocks.text: std/markdown -- parse a useful subset of Markdown into an AST, then render that AST to either a static HTML string or a `std/view` tree.

The point is content-heavy pages written in Markdown rather than hand-built views: author docs as text, `markdown.parse` them into blocks, and render the result wherever you need it. `to-html` gives a plain HTML string (server-side rendering, headless output); `to-view` gives a `view` that drops into a page and hydrates like anything else. Both walk the same AST, so the two renderings never drift.

The supported subset covers what documentation needs: ATX headings (each rendered with an `id` anchor derived from its text, so links can jump to it), paragraphs, fenced code blocks, blockquotes, bulleted and numbered lists (one level of nesting), GFM pipe tables, thematic breaks, and the inline run of bold/italic/code/links/images plus hard line breaks. It is deliberately forgiving rather than exhaustive -- anything it doesn't recognize as a marker is passed through as literal text, so parsing never fails.

It also supports fenced-div containers for styled callouts. A line opening with `:::` starts one and a bare `:::` line closes it; in between is ordinary Markdown, and containers nest. The opener's spec chooses the element: a bare word is the tag (so `::: aside` is a real `<aside>`; the tag defaults to `div`), each `.name` adds a CSS class, and `key="value"` adds an attribute. Several classes -- whether from `.name` shorthands or a `class="..."` attribute -- merge into one space-separated `class`.
renders `<aside class="callout warning" role="alert"> ... </aside>`.

An inline-level node: the contents of a paragraph, heading, or list item. A run of Markdown text parses into a `list inline`. Public (not opaque) so consumers can pattern-match the AST and render it however they like.

A block-level node. A whole document is a `list block`. `heading` carries its level (1-6); `code-block` its info/language string and verbatim body; the list variants carry one `list block` per item (so an item can hold several paragraphs or a nested list); `ordered-list` also carries its start number. `container` is the AST for a `::: tag .class key="value"` fenced div: a chosen HTML tag (so `::: aside` is an `<aside>`, not a `<div class="aside">`), a list of CSS classes, a list of passthrough attributes, and the nested blocks -- the home for callouts, asides, and warnings. `table` is a GFM pipe table: a header row (one `list inline` per cell) and the body rows (one cell list per row).
Parses Markdown text into a list of block nodes. Never fails: unrecognized markup degrades to plain text.
Renders an AST to a static HTML string -- server-side rendering and headless output. Reuses `std/view`'s renderer (and its HTML escaping), so the markup matches what `to-view` mounts in the browser.
Renders an AST to a `std/view` tree, wrapped in a single `<div>` so it composes as one node. The same content the browser mounts as live DOM.
Parse-then-render in one step: Markdown text straight to an HTML string.
Parse-then-render in one step: Markdown text straight to a `view`.
The plain text of an inline run, with all markup stripped -- the source for a heading's anchor or a table-of-contents entry.

A URL anchor for some text: lowercased, with every run of characters that aren't letters or digits collapsed to a single hyphen and the ends trimmed. So `"On this page!"` becomes `"on-this-page"`. Headings render with this as their `id`, so a table of contents links to `"#" ++ slug heading-text`.
Numbers and the usual math you reach for: rounding, square roots, logarithms, and trigonometry. The trig and log functions all work on `float`s.
Pluma keeps whole numbers (`int`) and decimals (`float`) separate and won't quietly mix them, so `to-float` and `to-int` are here for when you need to cross between the two.
The constant pi, 3.14159..., as a float.
Euler's number e, 2.71828..., the base of the natural logarithm.
Turns a whole number into a decimal. Exact for any int you're likely to use (up to about 9 quadrillion).
Chops the fractional part off a decimal, rounding toward zero. So 2.9 becomes 2, not 3 -- use `round` if you want the nearest whole number.
Square root. Gives `nan` ("not a number") for a negative input.
Absolute value -- the distance from zero, always positive. Works on ints.
Rounds down to the nearest whole number (toward negative infinity), returning an int.
Rounds up to the nearest whole number (toward positive infinity), returning an int.
Rounds to the nearest whole number, with exact halves going away from zero (2.5 becomes 3, -2.5 becomes -3).
Natural logarithm (base e) -- the inverse of `exp`.
Logarithm base 10. `log10 1000.0` asks "10 to what power is 1000?"
Logarithm base 2. `log2 8.0` asks "2 to what power is 8?"
e raised to the given power -- the inverse of `log`.
Sine of an angle measured in radians.
Cosine of an angle measured in radians.
Tangent of an angle measured in radians -- sine over cosine. Blows up toward infinity where `cos` crosses zero (odd multiples of pi/2).

items.name: handler
items.signature: alias handler fun http.request -> task http.response

blocks.text: A request handler: the shape `http.serve` and `router.handler` consume.

A wrapper around a handler. Build one as `fun next { fun req { ... } }`: act on `req` before calling `next` to do work on the way in, act on `next`'s response to do work on the way out, or skip `next` to end the request early.

items.signature: (list ((({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, method: method, path: string, query: string}) -> task ({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, status: int, stream-body: option (stream bytes)}) a) -> ({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, method: method, path: string, query: string}) -> task ({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, status: int, stream-body: option (stream bytes)}) a)) (({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, method: method, path: string, query: string}) -> task ({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, status: int, stream-body: option (stream bytes)}) a) -> ({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, method: method, path: string, query: string}) -> task ({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, status: int, stream-body: option (stream bytes)}) a

blocks.text: Wraps `base` in each middleware in `mws`. The first element is the *outermost* layer, so `chain [a, b] base` is `a (b base)`: a request flows in a -> b -> base, and the response flows back out base -> b -> a. An empty list returns `base` unchanged.
Tools for working with options -- chaining steps that might come up empty, and supplying fallbacks when one does.

The `option` type itself (`some` and `none`) is built in and always available, so you don't need this module just to make or match an option -- see the prelude. Import it (`use std/option`) for the helpers below.

Chains a follow-up step onto an option. `then opt f` runs `f` on the value inside `opt` when it's `some`, and `f` returns another option. If `opt` is already `none`, `f` is skipped and the whole thing is `none`. So you can line up several steps that each might come up empty, and the first `none` stops the rest.
Most of the time you'll reach for `try` instead, which reads top to bottom rather than nesting:

spans.text: # bail out with none if xs is empty
# otherwise carry on with the value

blocks.text: Returns the value inside a `some`, or falls back to whatever `fallback` produces when the option is `none`. The fallback is a function taking no arguments, and it only runs when it's actually needed.
This is exactly what the `??` operator does, so you'll usually write the operator and skip calling this directly:

spans.text: # the first element, or 0 if xs is empty

blocks.text: Spelled out the long way:

Returns the value inside a `some`, or stops the whole program when the option is `none`. The message you pass is written to standard error and the program exits nonzero (via `process.fail`). Reach for this in a script when an empty result leaves nothing sensible to do -- the recovering dual is `??`, which supplies a fallback instead of quitting.

spans.text: # some -> the contents; none -> "no home directory" on stderr, then exit

blocks.text: Transforms the value inside a `some` with `f`, leaving a `none` untouched. Use this when you have a plain transform (one that just returns a value, not another option) and want it to apply only when there's something there.
When `f` itself returns an option (so you'd otherwise get a nested `option (option b)`), reach for `then` instead, which flattens.
True when the option is `some` (holds a value), false when `none`.
True when the option is `none` (empty), false when `some`. The mirror of `is-some`.
Keeps a `some` only when its value passes `pred`; otherwise gives back `none`. A `none` stays `none`. Think of it as `filter` for a collection that holds at most one thing.
Collapses one layer of nesting: an `option (option a)` becomes an `option a`. `some (some x)` is `some x`; both `some none` and `none` become `none`.

Turns an option into a `result`, supplying the error to use when it's empty: `some v` becomes `ok v`, and `none` becomes `err e`. Handy at the boundary where "nothing here" needs to carry an explanation -- the dual is `result.ok`, which throws the error away again.

The types behind a project's `pluma.pa` file. Every Pluma project has one at its root that declares the project's name, version, and dependencies. The CLI finds it by looking for a top-level `def package :: package.info = { ... }`.
You don't usually name these types directly -- you just fill in a `package.info` record -- but here's what goes in one.

Where a dependency comes from. Most projects only ever need `simple`, which pins a version from the package registry. The rest cover a dependency with extra features switched on, one pulled straight from a git repository, or one living in a folder on your machine.
Each entry in a project's `dependencies` list pairs a name with one of these:

A project's metadata, as it appears in `pluma.pa`. Fill in every field: `name` and `version` are the ones that matter most, and the others can be empty strings or empty lists when you have nothing to put in them yet.
Adding a field here is a safe change; removing or renaming one is not, so it's done deliberately.
True when `p` is an absolute path (begins at the filesystem root, `/`).
The non-empty components of `p`, in order. Collapses repeated and trailing slashes, so `path.segments "/a//b/"` is `["a", "b"]`.
The final component of `p` -- the file or directory name. `path.filename "/a/b/c.pa"` is `"c.pa"`; an empty or root path gives `""`.
The parent directory of `p` -- everything before the final component. `path.parent "/a/b/c"` is `"/a/b"`; `path.parent "/a"` is `"/"`; a bare name like `"a"` gives `"."`.
The file extension of `p`, without the dot -- `some "pa"` for `"main.pa"`. `none` for a name with no extension, or a leading-dot dotfile like `".bashrc"`.
`p`'s final component with its extension stripped. `path.stem "/a/b.tar.gz"` is `"b.tar"` (only the last extension goes); a dotfile like `".bashrc"` is unchanged.
Joins `b` onto `a`, tidying the slashes between them. An absolute `b` replaces `a` entirely (`path.join "/a" "/b"` is `"/b"`); an empty side is dropped.
Joins a list of components into one path, left to right. `path.join-all ["a", "b", "c"]` is `"a/b/c"`.
`p` with its extension set to `ext` (given without a dot); an empty `ext` removes the extension. `path.with-extension "src/main.pa" "wasm"` is `"src/main.wasm"`.
Resolves `.` and `..` components and collapses redundant slashes -- a purely textual normalization, no filesystem access. `path.normalize "/a/b/../c"` is `"/a/c"`.

Queues: a first-in, first-out (FIFO) collection. You `enqueue` values onto the back and `dequeue` them from the front, so they come out in the order they went in -- like a line at a counter. Reach for one when order of arrival matters: work to process, a breadth-first search frontier, events to replay.

A queue is a *mutable* value, like a `list` grown with `push`/`pop`: `enqueue` and `dequeue` change the queue in place rather than returning a new one. The same aliasing caveat applies -- the change is visible through every binding pointing at the same queue -- so treat a queue as owned by the code that's filling and draining it. Use `to-list` to take a non-destructive snapshot.

Both `enqueue` and `dequeue` run in amortized O(1) time. Internally the queue keeps two lists: a `front` it pops the oldest element off of, and a `back` it pushes new elements onto. When the front runs dry it moves the back over in one reversing pass, so each element is handled a constant number of times across its lifetime.
A queue is two lists: `front` (popped from its end -- the oldest element) and `back` (pushed onto -- the newest). Dequeueing from an empty `front` first flips `back` into it (see `rebalance`).
An empty queue. The starting point you `enqueue` onto.
Builds a queue from a list, front of the list first (so `dequeue` returns the list's elements left to right).
Adds `x` to the back of the queue in place, in O(1).
Removes and returns the oldest element as `some oldest`, or `none` if the queue is empty. Mutates the queue in place, in amortized O(1).
Returns the oldest element as `some oldest` without removing it, or `none` if the queue is empty.
How many elements the queue holds. O(1).
Reports whether the queue has no elements.
The queue's elements as a list, oldest first, without changing the queue. The order matches the order `dequeue` would hand them back.

Random numbers, drawn from the operating system's secure source of randomness. That makes them safe even where unpredictability really matters -- passwords, tokens, session ids. Every call returns a fresh, independent result.
(A seedable generator -- the kind that replays the same "random" sequence every run, handy for tests and simulations -- isn't available yet, but is planned.)
A random whole number from 0 up to (but not including) 2^63. Draws fresh randomness each call.

spans.text: # a different large number every time

blocks.text: A random decimal from 0 up to (but not including) 1.
`n` random bytes, wrapped in `ok`. `err` if `n` is negative. Good for generating a key or a salt.
A random whole number from `low` up to (but not including) `high`, wrapped in `ok`. `err` if `low` isn't below `high`.

spans.text: # => ok n, where n is 1 through 6 (a die roll)

blocks.text: A random `true` or `false`, each with a 50% chance -- a coin flip.
Picks one element of the list at random, wrapped in `some`. `none` if the list is empty (there's nothing to pick).

Mutable cells. Most values in Pluma never change once created, which keeps code easy to follow. A `ref a` is the deliberate exception: a little box holding one value of type `a` that you can read, overwrite, or update in place. Reach for one when you genuinely need state that outlives a single expression -- a running counter, a cache you fill as you go.
`ref` is always available; you don't need to import it.
Makes a new cell holding `initial`.
Reads the current value out of a cell.
Overwrites the cell's value. Returns `nothing` -- read it back with `get` if you want to see the new value.
Reads the value, runs `f` on it, and writes the result back -- a read-modify-write in a single step. Returns `nothing`; read it back with `get` if you want the new value.

Searching and replacing text with regular expressions. A regex is a pattern describing a *shape* of text -- "one or more digits", "a word followed by an equals sign" -- rather than one exact string, so you can find or rewrite text you don't know ahead of time.

Pluma's patterns go between backticks and read a little differently from regexes elsewhere: exact text goes in double quotes, common character groups have plain names (`digit`, `letter`, `whitespace`), `+` means "one or more", and a piece you want to pull out later is a named capture written `<name: ...>`. So
matches text like "555-1234" and captures the two halves. The full pattern syntax lives in the language reference.
The `start` / `end` positions reported below are byte offsets into the string you searched.

A backtick literal reifies to the prelude `regex-pattern` tree (the `regex` type). The matcher translates that tree to an equivalent regex source and runs it on the host's `RegExp` engine, returning each match's byte offsets. The two strings cross to the host as latin1 (one byte = one code unit), so the engine's code-unit offsets coincide with byte offsets even for non-ASCII input; the character classes translate to ASCII byte ranges, so matching stays byte-level (Pluma indexes bytes, not codepoints).

A single match. `text` is the piece of the input that matched; `start` and `end` mark where it sat (as byte offsets). `groups` maps each named capture in the pattern to the text it grabbed. A named group that never fired -- because it was on the unused side of an "or", say -- is simply left out of the dict rather than mapped to "".
True when the regex matches anywhere in the input. Use this when you only care whether there's a match, not where it is or what it caught.

items.name: find
items.signature: regex-pattern string -> option ({end: int, groups: dict string string, start: int, text: string})

blocks.text: Finds the first match, scanning left to right, as a `match` record wrapped in `some`. `none` if the pattern doesn't match anywhere.

items.name: find-all
items.signature: regex-pattern string -> list ({end: int, groups: dict string string, start: int, text: string})

blocks.text: Finds every non-overlapping match, left to right, as a list of `match` records. Empty list if nothing matches.

spans.text: # collect just the matched text from each match

blocks.text: A shortcut for grabbing one named capture from the first match. `some text` when the regex matches and has a group by that name, `none` otherwise. Saves you unpacking a whole `match` record when you only want one piece.
Replaces every match with `replacement`. Inside `replacement`, drop in a captured group with `${name}`, and write a literal dollar sign as `$$`. References to a group that didn't match are dropped.
Like `replace`, but only touches the first match. Same `${name}` / `$$` replacement syntax.
Splits the input at every match, throwing the matched text away. A match at the start or end, or two in a row, leaves an empty string -- the same way `string.split` behaves.

Building the `request` value passed to a `remote` call. The `request` type itself is built in (see the prelude) and carries transport metadata -- the headers a client attaches and the server reads. Import this module (`use std/request`) for the builders below.

Identity is never set here. A client presents credentials (an `authorization` header or a cookie the transport attaches); the server derives and validates the authenticated user with a server-only query.
A fresh request with no extra metadata. The common authenticated call needs nothing more -- the transport attaches cookies on its own.
Attaches (or overwrites) one header on a request, returning the updated request. Use it for trace ids, idempotency keys, or a custom `authorization` header.
The headers attached to a request, as a plain dict. The transport reads this to turn metadata into outbound HTTP headers.
Tools for working with results -- chaining steps that might fail, and recovering when one does.

The `result` type itself (`ok` and `err`) is built in and always available, so you don't need this module just to make or match a result -- see the prelude. Import it (`use std/result`) for the helpers below.

items.name: then
items.signature: (result a b) (a -> result c b) -> result c b

blocks.text: Chains a follow-up step onto a result. `then r f` runs `f` on the value inside an `ok`, and `f` returns another result. If `r` is already `err`, `f` is skipped and that same error passes straight through. So a run of steps that each might fail stops at the first failure and carries its error out.
As with options, `try` is the readable way to write such a chain:

spans.text: # stop with the err if it fails

blocks.text: Returns the value inside an `ok`, or falls back to whatever `fallback` produces when the result is an `err`. The error value is discarded; if you need it, match on the result yourself instead. The fallback takes no arguments and only runs on `err`.
This is what `??` does on a result, so you'll usually write the operator:

spans.text: # the number, or 0 if it won't parse
