Returns the value inside an `ok`, or stops the whole program when the result is an `err`. The message you pass is written to standard error, followed by the error value itself, and the program exits nonzero (via `process.fail`). Reach for this in a script when there's nothing sensible to do but bail -- the recovering dual is `??`, which supplies a fallback instead of quitting.

spans.text: # ok -> the contents; err -> "reading config: <the os error>" then exit

items.name: map
items.signature: (result a b) (a -> c) -> result c b

blocks.text: Transforms the value inside an `ok` with `f`, leaving an `err` untouched (the error passes straight through). Use this for a plain transform that should only apply on success.
When `f` itself returns a result, reach for `then`, which flattens.

items.name: map-err
items.signature: (result a b) (b -> c) -> result a c

blocks.text: Transforms the error inside an `err` with `f`, leaving an `ok` untouched. The mirror of `map`: use it to translate one error type into another (say, wrapping a low-level os error in your own).
True when the result is `ok` (a success), false when `err`.
True when the result is `err` (a failure), false when `ok`. The mirror of `is-ok`.

Discards the error channel, keeping just the success as an option: `ok v` becomes `some v`, and any `err` becomes `none`. Use it when you stop caring *why* something failed and only want "did it produce a value?" -- the dual is `option.to-result`, which supplies an error.
(It can't be named `ok`, since that's the constructor it matches on.)

Keeps just the error as an option, discarding the success: `err e` becomes `some e`, and any `ok` becomes `none`. The mirror of `to-option` -- handy for collecting the failures out of a batch of results.
One endpoint: a rule for which requests it answers, paired with the handler that answers them. Opaque — build one with `get` / `post` / `prefix` and feed a list of them to `handler` or `serve`.

Inside, a route is just a function: given a request, it returns `some` of the handler's task when the request matches, or `none` when it doesn't. `handler` walks the list and runs the first `some` it finds.

items.name: get
items.signature: string (({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, method: method, path: string, query: string}) -> task ({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, status: int, stream-body: option (stream bytes)}) a) -> route

blocks.text: A route that answers GET requests whose path is exactly `path`.

items.name: post
items.signature: string (({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, method: method, path: string, query: string}) -> task ({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, status: int, stream-body: option (stream bytes)}) a) -> route

blocks.text: A route that answers POST requests whose path is exactly `path`.

items.name: prefix
items.signature: string (({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, method: method, path: string, query: string}) -> task ({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, status: int, stream-body: option (stream bytes)}) a) -> route

blocks.text: A route that answers any request whose path starts with `pfx`, regardless of method. Useful for handing a whole subtree to one handler — static files under `/static`, say — where the handler reads the rest of the path itself.

items.name: handler
items.signature: (list route) -> ({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, method: method, path: string, query: string}) -> task ({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, status: int, stream-body: option (stream bytes)}) a

blocks.text: Turns a list of routes into a single handler you can hand to `http.serve` (or mount under your own server). Routes are tried in order and the first match wins; if none match, the handler replies with a 404.

Binds to `address` and serves the routes forever — the one-liner for a router-only server. It's exactly `http.serve address (handler routes)`, so reach for `handler` instead when you want to mount the routes inside a server you're assembling yourself.

spans.text: # couldn't reach the server / connection dropped (detail)
# client built against a drifted endpoint (route)
# the server couldn't decode the arguments

blocks.text: Infrastructure failures of a remote call, carried on the (erased) task-failure channel and recovered typed via `task.attempt`. Domain errors stay inside the success payload (`task (result a e)`); these are the wire/transport faults that aren't any single endpoint's concern. Auth failures are infrastructure too -- 401/403 are cross-cutting and HTTP-shaped, so they ride here rather than every endpoint's payload.

Maps a non-200 status from a dispatcher into the typed `rpc-error` a caller recovers. The recognised statuses get their own case (so a client can branch on `unauthorized` without scraping a number); `route` names the endpoint for the 409 skew case; anything else is an opaque `server-fault`. Both transports share this so the status<->error mapping lives in exactly one place.

items.name: sender
items.signature: alias sender fun string bytes (dict string string) -> task bytes

blocks.text: A transport: one request/response round-trip. An implementation succeeds with the encoded reply bytes, or fails the task with an `rpc-error`. One transport per deploy target lives in its own module (`std/sys/rpc` / `std/web/rpc`) and installs itself as the ambient sender at startup (the build injects the call). Streaming is not covered here yet. (Cross-module `trait`/`implement` isn't supported, so the contract is a plain function type rather than a typeclass -- the transports are still ordinary hand-written Pluma, not compiler-synthesized.)

items.name: stream-sender
items.signature: alias stream-sender fun string bytes (dict string string) -> stream bytes

blocks.text: A streaming transport: opens a server->client subscription, surfacing each server-sent frame as one `bytes` element of a `stream`. End-of-stream and faults ride the stream the same way `std/stream` defines them (drained = the stream ends, a fault = a task failure on the puller). One per deploy target, installed alongside the unary `sender`. The streaming half of `sender`.

items.name: configure
items.signature: (string bytes (dict string string) -> task bytes a) -> nothing

blocks.text: Installs the ambient transport. A target's transport module calls this from its `install` (which the build runs before `main`); tests can call it to swap in a different transport.

items.name: configure-stream
items.signature: (string bytes (dict string string) -> stream bytes) -> nothing

blocks.text: Installs the ambient streaming transport (`stream R` endpoints). A target's transport module installs this alongside `configure` from its `install`.

The server origin the client posts to (`<origin>/rpc/<route>`), baked at build time from `pluma build/dev --server-url`. Empty = same-origin. Never settable at runtime: a client always talks to exactly the origin it was built for.

items.name: with-headers
items.signature: (list ((string, string))) (nothing -> task a b) -> task a b

blocks.text: Runs `body` with `headers` attached to every endpoint call inside it (the only thing the old `request` argument bought at a call site). Nested scopes merge, inner keys winning. Per-call concerns that aren't wire metadata already ride the `task` layer over a remote call for free -- deadline (`task.with-timeout`), retry (`task.retry`), cancellation (`scope`) -- so only headers need carrying here.

items.name: call-unary
items.signature: string string bytes -> task bytes rpc-error

blocks.text: Performs one unary call over the ambient transport: attaches the route's fingerprint plus any `with-headers` headers and round-trips the encoded body. The synthesized client stub wraps this with the wire encode/decode for the endpoint's types. Fails with `rpc-error` if no transport is installed.

Opens a subscription over the ambient streaming transport: the streaming dual of `call-unary`. Attaches the route's fingerprint plus any `with-headers` headers and hands back the frame stream; the synthesized client stub wraps this with the per-frame wire decode for the endpoint's result type. If no streaming transport is installed, a stream that faults on its first pull.

Lifts a `wire.decode` outcome into the task-failure channel: a malformed reply is a transport-level fault, not a value. The synthesized client stub calls this on the decoded result so a bad payload surfaces as a recoverable `rpc-error`.
Sets: an unordered collection of distinct values. `set a` is read "a set of `a`" -- `set int` is a set of whole numbers, `set string` a set of strings, and so on.

A set remembers *whether* a value is present, not how many times or in what order. Adding a value that's already there changes nothing, so a set never holds duplicates. Use one when you care about membership -- "have I seen this id?", "which tags are in use?" -- rather than position.

Elements can be any type that knows how to `hash` itself -- the built-in types (`int`, `float`, `string`, `bytes`, `bool`) all do, and your own enums can by `implement`ing the `hash` trait. The functions that look an element up (`insert`, `remove`, `contains`, `from-list`, the set-algebra functions) carry a `where (hash a)` constraint that says so.

A set is an immutable value, backed by the same persistent structure as `dict`: `insert`, `remove`, `union`, and friends return a *new* set and leave the original untouched, so two names for the same set never interfere.
Iteration order is unspecified: `to-list`, `map`, `filter`, `fold`, and `each` walk the elements in an arbitrary (hash-determined) order.
A set is a dict whose keys are the members; the value is a placeholder (`contains` only ever asks whether the key is present).
An empty set with no elements. The starting point you build up from with `insert`.
A set holding exactly one element.
Builds a set from a list, dropping duplicates.
Returns a new set with `x` added; the original is left unchanged. Adding an element that's already present returns an equivalent set.
Returns a new set without `x`; the original is left unchanged. Removing an element that isn't there returns an equivalent set.
Reports whether `x` is a member of the set.
How many elements the set holds.
Reports whether the set has no elements.
The set's elements as a list, in an arbitrary order.
The union of two sets: every element that's in either one.
The intersection of two sets: only the elements that are in both.
The difference of two sets: the elements of `a` that are not in `b`.
Reports whether every element of `a` is also in `b`.
Reports whether every element of `b` is also in `a` (the flip side of `subset-of`).
Reports whether two sets share no elements at all.
Reports whether two sets hold exactly the same elements.
Returns a new set with `f` applied to every element. Distinct elements can collide under `f`, so the result may be smaller than the input.
Returns a new set with only the elements for which `pred` answers true.

Combines the elements into a single value, starting from `acc` and folding in one element at a time. Order is unspecified, so prefer a `f` that doesn't depend on it (a commutative combine like `+`, `union`, `and`).
Runs `f` on every element for its side effect, discarding the results.
Reports whether any element satisfies `pred` (false for an empty set).
Reports whether every element satisfies `pred` (true for an empty set).
std/signal -- fine-grained reactivity: the core of the post-MVU frontend.

A `signal a` is a mutable cell that remembers who read it. A `computed` is a derived signal recomputed on demand. An `effect` is a side-effecting reader that re-runs whenever any signal it read changes. Together they form a dependency graph that updates exactly the parts that depend on what changed -- no central model, no message type, no whole-view re-render.

This module is platform-neutral -- it's just a graph over `ref`s, so it works server-side too. The view layer (`std/view`) binds a UI to signals on top of it, and `std/web/render` mounts that to the real DOM.

DESIGN. Tracking is *automatic*: `signal.get`, while an effect/computed is running, registers that reader as a subscriber (the "current observer" is held in a module `ref`, saved/restored around each run -- reactive runs never suspend, so a plain stack discipline is enough). Updates are *glitch-free* via a pull model: a `set` eagerly marks dependent computeds STALE and queues dependent effects, but computeds don't recompute until read -- so by the time an effect pulls a computed, every upstream is already fresh and each effect runs at most once per batch (see the diamond test).

Scoped teardown is via the owner tree (`owned`/`on-cleanup`): a dynamic subtree runs under a fresh owner so unmounting it disposes exactly its effects. Each `effect` also returns its own dispose function for standalone use.

`set` short-circuits on value equality (deep `==`): a write equal to the current value neither stores nor notifies, so a redundant `set` is free and an effect that writes back an unchanged value settles rather than looping. (A cycle that writes a genuinely *changing* value each pass still loops -- that's a real cycle.)

A reactive source. A plain cell has `owner` = none; a computed carries its recompute observer in `owner` so a reader can pull a fresh value first. id     -- source id, the key this source uses in an observer's `sources` value  -- current value (a cell's contents, or a computed's cache) subs   -- observer-id -> the observers currently reading this source stale  -- a computed whose inputs changed but hasn't recomputed (cells: false) owner  -- the recompute observer (computeds only; none for cells)
Coalesce many `set`s into a single round of effect runs. Re-entrant-safe: only the outermost `batch` triggers the drain.
A fresh read/write cell holding `initial`.
Install the post-flush hook (the web dev entry points this at its persist step).
Reset the per-render occurrence counters + observed-key log.
The structural keys derived since the last `reset-keys`, in creation order.
Capture every keyed signal's current value as `key -> bytes`, for persisting.
Install the previous instance's state, to seed signals as they're recreated. The web dev entry calls this (with the decoded dev-store blob) before the view is built.

Compiler-targeted constructor (hmr builds only). Like `new`, but stamped with its source call-site so a reload can match it to its previous-instance counterpart, seed it from the carried-over state, and register it for persist.
Read a signal. Inside an effect/computed this subscribes the reader, so it re-runs when the value changes. A stale computed is recomputed first (pull).
Read a signal *without* subscribing -- an escape hatch for reading current state inside an effect without depending on it. Still pulls a stale computed.

Write a cell and notify everything that depends on it. A write equal (by deep value `==`) to the current value is a no-op: it doesn't touch the cell or notify, so a redundant `set` costs nothing downstream and a self-referential effect (one that sets a signal it reads to an unchanged value) settles instead of looping.
Read-modify-write. Reads with `peek`, so calling `update` inside an effect doesn't make that effect depend on the signal.
Collects the teardown work for a reactive subtree, so the whole subtree can be disposed at once when it unmounts.
The owner-path prefix `new-keyed` stamps onto its keys.
Register a teardown function with the current owner (no-op outside an owner).

Run `body` under a fresh owner labelled `label`; return its result paired with a function that disposes every effect/computed/cleanup created under that owner. `label` extends the owner-path (a keyed-list row passes its key; a `show` passes "") so signals built inside `body` get path-prefixed, reorder-stable keys.
Run `body` without subscribing the current observer to anything it reads -- so building a dynamic subtree inside an effect doesn't make the outer effect depend on that subtree's signals.
A derived, read-only signal. `body` is run once now to seed the cache and discover its dependencies; thereafter it's marked stale on any upstream change and recomputed lazily the next time it's read.
Run `body` now, and again whenever any signal it read changes. Returns a dispose function that unsubscribes the effect so it stops reacting.

A single cell's value. SQLite stores exactly five classes and decides a cell's class per *value*, not per column, so this mirrors that reality verbatim. Note there is no `bool`: SQLite has no boolean storage class. Booleans are an interpretation of `int` (see `sql.bool` / `sql.get-bool`).
Smart constructors so callers write `sql.int 5` rather than the qualified variant `sql.value.int 5`. `sql.bool` encodes a boolean the way SQLite expects: 1 or 0.
A REAL value: `sql.float 1.5`.
A TEXT value: `sql.text "ada"`.
A BLOB value from raw bytes.
The SQL NULL value.
A boolean, stored as 1 or 0 (SQLite has no boolean type).
One result row, addressed by column name. Opaque: `std/sys/db` builds these from a query result (via `row-of`); you read them through `cell` or, better, the typed `get-*` accessors below.
Builds a row from its `(name, value)` cells. Used by `std/sys/db` to lift a host result across the boundary; rarely needed directly, but handy for testing a decoder.
The raw value in column `name`, or `value.null` if the row has no such column. Prefer the `get-*` accessors, which also check the value's class.

Typed accessors. Each reads one column and checks its storage class, returning a named `err` (not a bare `none`) when the column is missing or the wrong class -- so a decode failure tells you *which* column and *what* was expected. Thread them with `try`:
Reads a TEXT column, or an `err` naming the column if it's missing or not text.
Accepts a stored `int` too, promoting it -- SQLite may hand back `3` where you expected `3.0`, and refusing that is more annoying than useful.
Reads a BLOB column, or an `err` naming the column if it's missing or not a blob.
Reads an INTEGER column as a boolean: 0 is false, anything else is true (SQLite's own convention, since it has no boolean type).

items.signature: row string (row string -> result a string) -> result (option a) string

blocks.text: Lifts any accessor over a nullable column: a NULL cell reads as `ok none`, otherwise the inner accessor runs and its value is wrapped in `some`.

A single WHERE condition. Built through the smart constructors below (`sql.eq`, ...) so callers never write the qualified variant. `one-of` lowers to `IN (...)`; `like` takes a SQL LIKE pattern. (The comparison variants are spelled out -- `equal`/`greater`/`less` rather than `eq`/`gt`/`lt` -- so they don't clash with the built-in `ordering` enum's `lt`/`eq`/`gt`, which the `compare` codegen resolves by variant name.)
Matches rows where the column equals the value: `sql.eq "id" (sql.int 5)`.
Matches rows where the column does not equal the value.
Matches rows where the column is greater than the value.
Matches rows where the column is greater than or equal to the value.
Matches rows where the column is less than the value.
Matches rows where the column is less than or equal to the value.
Matches a SQL LIKE pattern: `sql.like "name" "ada%"`.
Matches rows whose column is one of the values (SQL `IN`).

Boolean combinators over predicates, so a filter is a tree, not just a flat AND-list. `all` AND-joins, `any` OR-joins, `not` negates -- each parenthesized when it lowers, so precedence is explicit. An empty `all` is `1` (TRUE -- matches every row); an empty `any` is `0` (FALSE -- matches none), mirroring the empty-`one-of` convention. A top-level `filter` list is still AND-joined, so these only matter when you need OR or NOT *inside* a condition:
Matches when any of the given predicates hold (SQL `OR`).
Negates a predicate (SQL `NOT`).
A single ORDER BY term: a column and a direction. (Named `sort`, not `ordering`, because `ordering` is the built-in comparison-result enum.)
Sorts ascending by a column: `sql.asc "name"`.
Sorts descending by a column: `sql.desc "created"`.

1. Every `value` leaves only in the bind list; the SQL text holds a `?` for it. Nothing
