The `
` element: a line break.
The `<wbr>` element: a point where the text may break if needed.
The `<ins>` element: text inserted into the document.
The `<del>` element: text deleted from the document.
The `` element: an image.
The `<iframe>` element: another page embedded in an inline frame.
The `<embed>` element: external application or interactive content.
The `<object>` element: external content such as an image, page, or plugin.
The `<param>` element: a parameter for its object element.
The `<video>` element: a video player.
The `<audio>` element: an audio player.
The `<source>` element: a media source for video, audio, or picture.
The `<track>` element: a text track such as captions or subtitles.
The `<canvas>` element: a bitmap canvas for drawing with scripts.
The `<picture>` element: a container offering several image sources.
The `<table>` element: tabular data.
The `<caption>` element: a caption for its table.
The `<colgroup>` element: a group of columns in a table.
The `<col>` element: a column (or span of columns) in a table.
The `<tbody>` element: the body rows of a table.
The `<thead>` element: the header rows of a table.
The `<tfoot>` element: the footer rows of a table.
The `<tr>` element: a row of table cells.
The `<td>` element: a data cell in a table.
The `<th>` element: a header cell in a table.
The `<form>` element: an interactive form.
The `<fieldset>` element: a group of related form controls.
The `<legend>` element: a caption for its fieldset.
The `<label>` element: a caption for a form control.
The `<input>` element: a form input control.
The `<button>` element: a clickable button.
The `<select>` element: a drop-down list of options.
The `<datalist>` element: a list of suggested values for an input.
The `<optgroup>` element: a group of options in a select.
The `<option>` element: an option in a select or datalist.
The `<textarea>` element: a multi-line text input.
The `<output>` element: the result of a calculation or user action.
The `<progress>` element: a progress bar.
The `<meter>` element: a scalar measurement within a known range.
The `<details>` element: a disclosure widget that opens and closes.
The `<summary>` element: the visible heading for its details widget.
The `<dialog>` element: a dialog box or modal.
The `<menu>` element: a menu of commands.
The `class` attribute: the CSS classes applied to the element.
The `id` attribute: a unique identifier for the element.
The `style` attribute: inline CSS as a raw string (see `style-of` for a typed `std/css` declaration list).

A conditional `class` attribute: keep each class whose flag is true. `view.classes [("btn", true), ("active", signal.get on)]` => `class="btn active"` when `on`. For a *reactive* class that re-renders as a signal changes, wrap the string form in `bind`: `view.bind-attr "class" (fun { view.class-list [...] })`.
The space-joined class string from `(name, on)` pairs -- the value behind `classes`, exposed for use inside a reactive `bind`.

A typed inline `style` attribute from a `std/css` declaration list: `view.style-of [css.color (css.hex "#e11"), css.padding (css.rem 1.0)]`. For a reactive style, wrap `css.inline` in `bind`: `view.bind-attr "style" (fun { css.inline [...] })`.

Apply an extracted `css.ruleset` as a `class` attribute: yields the rule's hashed class name and registers its CSS in `std/css`'s collector, so server-side `css.style-tag`/`collect` (or a build-time extraction) emits exactly the rules a render actually used. The full power of CSS -- `:hover`, `@media`, etc. -- that an inline `style` can't express.

Apply an extracted `css.ruleset` reactively: its hashed class is on the element while `cond ()` holds and removed when it doesn't, re-evaluated when the signals `cond` reads change. The reactive companion to `styled` -- the idiomatic way to express a state-dependent style (`.active`, `.done`) without an inline `style` string. Composes with `styled` and other class attributes on the same node: all of them merge into one `class` (like `style`/`css-var` already do), so a base rule and any number of conditional ones stack. The rule is registered up front (so the stylesheet always carries it), only its presence on the element is conditional.

A reactive CSS custom property on this element: `view.css-var "accent" (fun { theme.color (signal.get theme) })` sets `--accent`, re-applying when its signals change. This is the dynamic boundary: a static extracted `css.rule` reads the value with `css.var "accent"`, and this supplies the live value -- so the rule stays cacheable while the value tracks a signal. (Named `css-var`, not `var`, since `var` is the `<var>` element builder.) Multiple custom properties on one node merge into its `style`; on the server `to-string` renders the initial value into the inline style, and the client re-applies it via `node.style.setProperty`.
The `title` attribute: advisory text, usually shown as a tooltip.
The `lang` attribute: the language of the element's content.
The `dir` attribute: the writing direction of the element's content.
The `role` attribute: the ARIA role describing the element's purpose.
The `tabindex` attribute: the element's place in the keyboard tab order.
The `href` attribute: the URL a link points to.
The `src` attribute: the URL of an embedded resource.
The `alt` attribute: alternative text describing an image.
The `rel` attribute: how a linked resource relates to this page.
The `target` attribute: where to open a link, such as "_blank".
The `width` attribute: the element's width.
The `height` attribute: the element's height.
The `placeholder` attribute: hint text shown in an empty input.
The `input-type` attribute: the kind of input. Sets the HTML `type`; named `input-type` because `type` is a keyword.
The `name` attribute: the field name submitted with the value.
The `for` attribute: the id of the form control a label describes.
The `action` attribute: the URL a form submits to.
The `method` attribute: the HTTP method a form submits with.
The `min` attribute: the smallest allowed value.
The `max` attribute: the largest allowed value.
The `step` attribute: the spacing between allowed values.
The `pattern` attribute: a regular expression the value must match.
The `accept` attribute: the file types a file input accepts.
The `autocomplete` attribute: how the browser should autofill the field.
The `cols` attribute: the visible width of a textarea, in characters.
The `rows` attribute: the visible number of lines in a textarea.
The `colspan` attribute: how many columns a table cell spans.
The `rowspan` attribute: how many rows a table cell spans.
The `disabled` attribute: whether the control is disabled.
The `required` attribute: whether the control must be filled in before submit.
The `selected` attribute: whether the option starts out selected.
The `readonly` attribute: whether the value can be edited.
The `multiple` attribute: whether several values are allowed.
The `autofocus` attribute: whether the control is focused when the page loads.
The `hidden` attribute: whether the element is hidden from rendering.
std/web/dom -- building and reacting to a web page's DOM, from the browser.

These functions only exist when you compile for the browser target (`pluma build --web`): they reach the real DOM through the host page, so they're gated on the `Dom` capability (see compiler/src/platform.rs) and are unavailable on the server.

A `node` is a live handle to one DOM element or text node. You make nodes (`create-element`, `create-text`), wire them together (`append-child`), give them content and attributes (`set-text`, `set-attribute`), and attach behaviour (`on-click`). `body ()` hands back the page's root, the node everything else hangs under. A node is opaque -- you hold the handle and pass it along, but can't look inside.
Most apps build their UI with `std/view` + `std/web/render` (the signals frontend) and never touch this module directly.

A live handle to one DOM node. Opaque: the browser owns it, you hold the handle. (The runtime carrier is an engine `externref`, not the `int` below -- the payload is a formality so the type is a real opaque enum.)
The page's root node -- the `<body>` everything else is appended under.
Make a fresh element node of the given tag, detached from the page until you `append-child` it somewhere.
Make a fresh text node holding the given string.
Append `child` as the last child of `parent`.
Insert `new` as a child of `parent`, right before the existing child `before`. The building block for inserting into the middle of a list.
Remove `child` from `parent`.
Replace `old` (a child of `parent`) with `new`, in place.
Set an attribute (`class`, `href`, `value`, ...) on a node.
Remove an attribute from a node.

Set a CSS custom property on a node's inline style -- `node.style.setProperty`. The name carries its leading `--` (`set-style-property node "--accent" "#e11"`). Distinct from `set-attribute "style"`, which would clobber the whole declaration block; this writes one property, so several can coexist. The renderer uses it to apply a reactive `view.css-var`.

Set a DOM *property* on a node -- `node[name] = value` -- as distinct from an attribute (`set-attribute`). The difference matters for *live* state: an `<input>`'s `value` attribute is only its initial text, while the `value` property is what the user is currently editing, so a controlled input must set the property to push the model's value back into the field.

Set a boolean DOM property -- `node[name] = flag` -- e.g. `checked`, `disabled`, `selected`. A bool, not a string, because `node.disabled = "false"` would be truthy; pass the flag and the host assigns a real boolean.
Replace a node's text content with the given string.
Read an input node's current `.value` (the empty string if it has none).

Read the text content of the element with the given id (the empty string if there is no such element). Distinct from `get-value`, which reads a form control's live value; this reads any element's text -- most usefully a server-rendered boot payload (`<script id="..." type="application/pluma">...</script>`), the channel an SSR'd page uses to hand the client its initial data so hydration needs no refetch. `std/web/render`'s `boot-data` wraps this with the hex + `wire` decode.

The `i`-th child node of `parent` (0-based, in document order). Used by `render.hydrate` to walk a server-rendered tree alongside the `view`: the view tree and the SSR'd DOM are 1:1 in document order, so child `i` of an element's view children is `child-at node i`. (Out of range yields a null handle -- the hydrator only indexes positions it knows the server emitted.)

Run `handler` whenever the named DOM event (`"click"`, `"input"`, `"submit"`, ...) fires on the node. The handler receives the `event`. Returns a *handler id* -- an opaque int naming the registry slot the handler lives in. Pass it to `set-handler` to re-point the listener in place without detaching the node; ignore it for a one-off listener.

Replace the handler at an existing handler id (from `add-listener`) in place, without touching the live DOM listener -- the next time the event fires, the new handler runs. Lets the runtime re-point a reused node's behaviour.

Dev-only key/value store, backed by the page's `localStorage`. `pluma dev`'s hot-reload uses these to carry keyed signal state across a full reload (see `render.mount-dev`); they are not part of the app-authoring API. `dev-store-get` returns `""` when the key is absent.
Reads a dev-store value, or `""` if the key is absent (see `dev-store-set`).
Sugar for the common case: run `handler` on every click.
The current URL's path (`location.pathname`), e.g. `"/items/42"`. The entry point of a multi-page client uses it to pick which page to hydrate.
The query string with its leading `?` (`location.search`), or `""` if absent.
The fragment with its leading `#` (`location.hash`), or `""` if absent.

Navigate to `url` by pushing a new history entry, *without* a page reload (`history.pushState`). The app re-renders the new route itself; pair with `on-pop-state` so the browser's back/forward buttons stay in sync.
Replace the current history entry's URL with `url`, no reload (`history.replaceState`) -- a navigation that leaves no back-button step.

Run `handler` on every back/forward navigation (the `popstate` event). The handler reads the new URL with `path ()` and re-renders accordingly. The listener lives for the page; its event carries no payload a handler needs.
std/web/fetch -- the browser's HTTP transport for RPC client stubs.

In the browser there are no raw TCP sockets, so a remote call can't ride `std/sys/net`/`std/sys/http` the way the server and native clients do. Instead it goes through the host page's `fetch`. In the browser, both `post` (one reply) and `fetch-stream` (many) ride the **same host-fed pull channel**: the host starts an async `fetch` and pushes the reply(s) into a wasm channel, and a parked puller drains them -- so a request *suspends* the calling fiber rather than blocking the main thread, and many can be in flight at once. `post` is the single-shot degenerate case: the host pushes one reply, then a clean end. (Under the V8 sys host -- the dev/test stand-in -- `post` is instead a blocking exchange; same type, different lowering. `fetch-stream` is browser-only.)

This module only exists in a web build (the `web-fetch` built-in is gated to the web target, like `std/web/dom`). It is the browser HTTP transport for RPC; the sys arm is `http.fetch`. The result shape matches `http.fetch`'s so a generated RPC stub reads `.status` and `.body` the same way on both targets.
A reply from the server: the HTTP status code and the raw response body. (Same structural shape as `http.response`; headers aren't surfaced -- an RPC stub only needs the status and body.)

items.name: post
items.signature: string (dict string string) bytes -> task ({body: bytes, headers: dict string string, status: int}) string

blocks.text: POSTs `body` to `url` with `headers`, returning the server's response. A transport or decode error *fails* the task. The web counterpart of `http.fetch ... post ...`.

items.name: fetch-stream
items.signature: string (dict string string) bytes -> stream bytes

blocks.text: Subscribe to `url` with `headers`, POSTing `body`; each server SSE frame surfaces as one `bytes` element. The release (`close`) runs on every terminus -- drained, faulted, or cancelled. The web counterpart of `http.fetch-stream ... post ...`.
std/web/render -- mount a `std/view` tree into the live browser DOM.

The browser-side consumer of a `view` (its server-side dual is `view.to-string`). `mount` walks the view once, builds the real DOM via `std/web/dom`, and wires every reactive part to a `signal.effect` so the DOM stays in sync surgically -- there is no diff and no retained virtual tree. After mount, updates flow signal -> effect -> DOM directly.
A signals app's `main` *is* the mount:

A pure client-only app (no SSR) is exactly this: `mount` builds the DOM and, since no server emitted a `<style>`, also installs the runtime stylesheet (see `sync-styles`), so collector-backed `view.styled` classes get their CSS.
`mount` returns `task nothing` -- the browser entry seeds and pumps it, then the instance stays alive to service DOM events.

Reactive control flow (`view.show` / `view.for`) is realised here: each gets a `display: contents` host element it manages, an effect that re-runs when its condition / list changes, and a `signal.owned` scope per mounted subtree so unmounting disposes exactly that subtree's effects (and a `signal.on-cleanup` so an ancestor unmount tears the dynamic content down too). v1 uses a wrapper host rather than Solid-style comment anchors -- a later refinement.

Mount the app under `parent`. The root view is a zero-argument function (as in SolidJS's `render(() => App(), root)`) -- so the dev variant can restore saved state before any signal is constructed. Returns `task nothing` so the browser entry can seed and pump it; the wasm instance then stays alive to dispatch DOM events.

`mount`, plus keyed-signal persistence across a `pluma dev` reload. Restore runs before `v ()` so `new-keyed` seeds from the carried-over state; the post-flush hook re-persists after each change, and we persist once now to capture the initial state.
--- hydrate: adopt a server-rendered DOM tree instead of building it ---------

The third `view` consumer (after `mount` = build DOM, `view.to-string` = render HTML). `hydrate` walks the `view` alongside the DOM the *server* already produced and **adopts** each existing node -- attaching effects to reactive text/attributes and listeners to handlers -- instead of creating new ones. The static structure is reused untouched: no flash, no re-render. The view tree and the SSR'd DOM are 1:1 in document order, so children line up by index.

`mount` appends the root view as `parent`'s single child, so the server's first child of `parent` is the root view's node. (The server's initial signal state is threaded in so client and server agree before `v ()` runs.)

v1 limit: two adjacent `view.text`/`text-of` siblings collapse into one DOM text node when the HTML is parsed, breaking the 1:1 index alignment -- wrap reactive text in an element (`view.span [] [view.text-of ...]`), as the examples do.
--- boot payload: read the server's serialized initial data ------------------

The companion to hydration's data side. A server-rendered page renders its view from some data `D`; for the client to hydrate the *same* tree it needs the same `D`. Rather than refetch `D` over RPC (a wasteful round-trip, and a window where the page is visible but not yet interactive), the server embeds it once -- as `hex.encode (wire.encode D)` inside a non-executed `<script id="..." type="application/pluma">` -- and the client reads it back here, synchronously, before building the view. `id` is that script's id.

Returns `err` when the element is absent or the bytes don't decode (e.g. a page served as a plain client-only shell), so the caller can fall back to fetching -- the same app then works whether or not it was server-rendered.
Installs this transport (unary + streaming) as the ambient sender. The build runs it before `main` on a web client.
Running a compiled Pluma program inside the browser.

The companion to `compile.to-wasm-hex` (`std/sys/compile`): give this the hex string that compile produced and it instantiates the module right here in the page, runs it, and hands back whatever the program printed. Both standard output and standard error are captured into the returned string.

Only the console surface is wired up -- printing, math, and the like. File, network, database, and DOM access are not available to a sandboxed program, so one that reaches for them comes back with `err` describing what was missing. This is the engine behind the playground's "Run" button.

Runs the wasm module described by `hex` (as produced by `compile.to-wasm-hex`) and returns everything it printed. `err` if the module can't be instantiated or the program stops with a failure partway through.
Writes `token` into the page's URL fragment (so the link reproduces whatever the token encodes) and copies the resulting absolute URL to the clipboard. Backs the playground's "Share" button.
